name: The anointing at Bethany
notes: A threefold parallel of the anointing of Jesus at Bethany, told by Matthew, Mark, and John. The shared core is one and the same occasion: a costly ointment of spikenard poured out upon Jesus, the disciples' objection that it might have been sold for the poor, Jesus' defense of the woman, and his word that the deed prepared him for his burial. Matthew and Mark give the broad vantage (the house of Simon the leper, the alabaster box poured on his head, and the promise that the deed will be told for a memorial wherever the gospel is preached), while John supplies complementary detail from up close (the supper where Lazarus sat, that the woman was Mary anointing Jesus' feet and wiping them with her hair, and that the objector was Judas Iscariot). The head-anointing of Matthew/Mark and the feet-anointing of John are complementary vantages of the abundant ointment, not competing reports. John records no memorial saying, which simply reads in place. The anointing in Luke 7 by a sinful woman in the house of a Pharisee in Galilee is a separate occasion and is intentionally not included here.
